title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kenai, Russian rivers are just the beginning for anglers By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21st, 2005 Last Modified: April 22nd, 2005 at 09:02 PM You can feel it as you pass the guide shacks and fly shops of Cooper Landing, hear it in the upper Kenai River as it carries the drift-boat fleets past the Russian River ferry. These things, along with the smell of campfire smoke, mountain winds and salmon that arrive in waves all summer long, leave no doubt that this is an anglers' place. Set in the heart of the Kenai Peninsula, among mountain ranges where rivers are born, Cooper Landing, a rural community about 100 miles south of Anchorage on the Sterling Highway, is the gateway to some of Alaska's finest, most accessible fishing. From here the action begins along the banks of …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Angling paradise: Seward has a line on halibut and salmon fishing By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21st, 2005 Last Modified: April 22nd, 2005 at 01:36 AM Off the stern of the charter boat Glacier Breeze, the peaks of the Aialik Peninsula jutted from the sea like the teeth of a giant salmon shark. Puffins, gulls, murres and auklets flew overhead and, in the distance, Bear Glacier could be seen winding out of the Kenai Mountains, pouring its icy heart into Resurrection Bay. In the center of it all, angler Cheryl Myers had barely dropped her herring bait over the gunwale when she suddenly cried out. An unseen force was trying to wrench the salmon rod from her hands, forcing the retiree from Apple Valley, Calif., to hold on as the tip bent sharply toward the water. Skipper Ch…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing fever: Early-season anglers converge on the lower Kenai Peninsula By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21st, 2005 Last Modified: April 22nd, 2005 at 01:36 AM Sometime around mid-April, as winter softens into spring, salmon-hungry Southcentral anglers look first to the sea. Specifically, they head for the lower Kenai Peninsula, where early-run kings start appearing off the beaches of places like Anchor Point, Whiskey Gulch and Deep Creek. Launching skiffs and small cabin cruisers into the surf, these early-bird anglers patrol the shorelines, working off the kelp beds where bait fish gather. This is a troll fishery, and cut-plug herring, spoons and large spinners all take their share of bright, springtime fish. The salmon during these early-season days come in fits and …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing for fun: Kachemak Bay's waters are full of halibut and salmon By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21st, 2005 Last Modified: April 22nd, 2005 at 01:36 AM The five-hour, 225-mile drive south from Anchorage to Homer is more scenic pleasure than long haul. Time passes quickly in a collage of mountain ranges, hanging glaciers, alpine passes and wildlife. And along the way, anglers' hearts beat a little faster as the Sterling Highway crosses the famous Kenai, Ninilchik and Anchor rivers. Then, in its final miles, the road swings west toward the edge of a high bluff to reveal a stunning view of one of Southcentral's crown jewels: Kachemak Bay. A mountain-flanked region of cold, clean marine waters and remote beaches, Kachemak not only promises the fishing trip of a lifetime…

title: Kenai, Russian rivers are just the beginning for anglers
summary: You can feel it as you pass the guide shacks and fly shops of Cooper Landing, hear it in the upper Kenai River as it carries the drift-boat fleets past the Russian River ferry. These things, along with the smell of campfire smoke, mountain winds and salmon that arrive in waves all summer long, leave no doubt that this is an anglers' place. Set in the heart of the Kenai Peninsula, among mountain ranges where rivers are born, Cooper Landing, a rural community about 100 miles south of Anchorage on the Sterling Highway, is the gateway to some of Alaska's finest, most accessible fishing. From here the action begins along the banks of the upper Kenai and Russian rivers. Famous for huge salmon runs and excellent fishing for rainbow trout and Dolly Varden, these easily reached streams are located …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Southeast Alaska: Off-the-roadway fishing at its finest By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 21st, 2005 Last Modified: April 22nd, 2005 at 01:36 AM I discovered Southeast Alaska as a fly-fisherman many years ago while searching for steelhead on Prince of Wales Island. A friend and I had paid a Wrangell bush pilot to drop us off at a U.S. Forest Service cabin we'd reserved on a remote lake and stream system. For three late-April days we explored the country and its water, casting for steelhead in the stream and learning what we could about the region. Along the way we encountered rufous hummingbirds no larger than a man's thumb and old-growth forests with tree trunks the size of living rooms. Black bears and mink ran the stream banks, and the bones of salmon from the previous …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Rabid Iditarod critic foams at the mouth Published: April 17th, 2005 Last Modified: April 17th, 2005 at 04:17 AM "No matter how skillfully propaganda is presented, it will not lead to success unless a fundamental principle is considered with continually sharp attention: It has to confine itself to little and to repeat this eternally.'' -- Adolph Hitler Enter Margery Glickman. Glickman, for those of you who don't already know, is the Florida woman who bills herself as the "Sled Dog Action Coalition.'' Since the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race ended in March, she has been firing off communiques to newspapers across the country, pounding away at the theme that celebrity musher Rachael Scdoris should be taken to task for "forcing'' a dog team 700 miles up the Iditarod Trail to Eagle…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: The Seward Silver Salmon Derby is 50 By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: April 15th, 2005 Last Modified: April 16th, 2005 at 10:01 PM Cheryl Myers of Apple Valley, Calif., stepped aboard the charter boat Glacier Breeze on a Sunday morning last July quietly wondering what the day might bring. Retired and enjoying her first trip to Alaska, she had never in her life caught a silver salmon. A few minutes spent fishing the salty green swells of Resurrection Bay would quickly change that. About an hour's run from the Seward small-boat harbor, Breeze skipper Chad Washburn, a slight and sprightly 21-year-old with curly red hair and seemingly boundless energy, cut the engine outside Agnes Cove. Lit by a low morning sun, the skies overhead were bright and still. The waters beneath the boat,…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Outdoors hot line Published: April 17th, 2005 Last Modified: April 17th, 2005 at 04:17 AM BOATING Coast Guard Auxiliary offers free vessel checks Ensure your boat is seaworthy with free vessel checks offered by the U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary. The auxiliary will verify that your boat's safety equipment is up to date, working and in compliance with federal and state regulations. Exams take place today at the Kenai Sports Show; 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. Saturday at the West Marine parking lot; May 5 at Deep Creek; May 21 in Fairbanks and the Fred Meyer parking lot in Eagle River; May 21 and 22 in Whittier; and May 27-30 at Big Lake. To schedule an exam, call one of the following numbers: Anchorage and Whittier, 522-5052; Eagle River, 696-2570; Fairbanks, 1-907-479-3531; Homer, 1-907-235-5479; Kenai, …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Iditarod pumps up Nome's lean winter CASH: Soaring fuel prices make dependable infusion especially helpful this year. By MARGARET BAUMAN Alaska Journal of Commerce Published: March 26th, 2005 Last Modified: March 26th, 2005 at 04:40 AM Iditarod dog teams mushing under the finish-line arch in Nome are a welcome sight for race fans and a $1.1 million shot in the arm for Nome merchants. "Iditarod and its associated events is pretty much the show in March," said Randy Romenesko, Nome city manager. Bed taxes bring in an extra $109,000 in March, said Cussy Kauer of the city's finance department, who has seen the finish of every single Iditarod race. "The tills are smoking up here," said Kauer, the great-granddaughter of a 1890s Gold Rush adventurer. That financial boost is especially important t…

summary: This week's stories: Mat-Su Alaska Outdoor Adventure Show Don't miss this Alaska event, a first for the Mat-Su area, geared toward the outdoor enthusiast and featuring more than 150,000 square feet of sports- and adventure-themed booths, hundreds of vendor displays and hourly educational seminars. Meet hunting and fishing guides, learn outdoor skills and discover exclusive lodges and (More...) Having a whale of a time Tours capture a glimpse of grays as they migrate back to Alaska SEWARD -- It's surprising how excited a group of grown-ups can become at the sight of a mottled black-and-gray hump peeking out of the ocean more than 200 feet away. Without the aid of binoculars, the hump looked like nothing more than flotsam, perhaps an old piece of driftwood taken (More...) Be prepared for col…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Hunters show interest in bear refuges GAME BOARD: Famous McNeil River bruins are at risk, critics say, as panel mulls opening adjacent lands to hunting. By TOM KIZZIA Anchorage Daily News Published: March 4th, 2005 Last Modified: March 4th, 2005 at 05:03 AM Click to enlarge Click to enlarge HOMER -- Rod Arno remembers guiding bear hunters in the country south of Kamishak Bay. It was the only place in his career where he led a client to a coastal brown bear and then, the next day, returned with another client to shoot a second trophy feeding on the unsalvaged remains of the first one. That state land around the Douglas River was closed to hunting 20 years ago because a land trade with the National Park Service was under discussion. But the trade never took place, and now the state Board of …

title: Mat-Su Alaska Outdoor Adventure Show
summary: Don't miss this Alaska event, a first for the Mat-Su area, geared toward the outdoor enthusiast and featuring more than 150,000 square feet of sports- and adventure-themed booths, hundreds of vendor displays and hourly educational seminars. Meet hunting and fishing guides, learn outdoor skills and discover exclusive lodges and wilderness camps; get deals on recreational vehicles, boats, snow machines, camping equipment and more. Entrance fee: $6. Free parking. Friday: noon-8 p.m. Saturday: 10 a.m.-9 p.m. Sunday: noon-6 p.m. Wasilla Multi-Use Sports Complex, 1001 S. Mack Drive, Wasilla. (277-7469 to reserve a vendor booth; 277-7469 or 562-8640 for information)

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Swingley's dog dies after being dropped from team Published: March 18th, 2005 Last Modified: March 18th, 2005 at 03:21 PM A dog dropped from the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race in the Bering Sea coast village of Elim Tuesday morning died in Anchorage on Thursday, according to the Iditarod Trail Commitee. The animal from the team of four-time Iditarod champ Doug Swingley from Lincoln, Mont., was thought to be suffering from pneumonia, according to a press release from the Iditarod. Swingley turned the dog over to a veterinarian in Elim about 8 a.m. Tuesday. The 2-year-old female named Nellie was flown to Nome the next day, according to the Iditarod. It spent most of the day there before being flown to Anchorage for what the race called "follow-up care.'' The dog died in Anchorage at 5 a.m. Thu…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Bison hunt allows lucky few a peek into Alaska's history DELTA: Transplanted herd a reminder of extinct native species. By RILEY WOODFORD The Associated Press Published: February 27th, 2005 Last Modified: February 27th, 2005 at 01:42 AM The three animals were all big, but one shaggy bull dusted with snow stood out. Hunter Travis Smith, 14, knelt and rested the heavy octagon barrel of his buffalo rifle on a shooting stick, then took aim at a 1,200-pound Alaska bison. A herd of about 400 wild, free-ranging plains bison roams the countryside near Delta Junction. About 16,000 hunters apply each year for the chance to hunt these massive beasts, and only 130 people received permits last year. Smith was one of the lucky few to draw a permit and headed 500 miles north to Delta from his home in Ken…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: March 27th, 2005 Last Modified: March 27th, 2005 at 05:24 AM BOATING Kayak trip raises funds for conservation group The second-annual Paddle for Marine Conservation is May 22-28, and reservations for the seven-day Prince William Sound kayak trip are being accepted. Proceeds support the Alaska Marine Conservation Council, a nonprofit working to protect the health of Alaska marine ecosystems. The itinerary will focus on humpback whales and glaciers. The minimum donation is $1,499 per person and includes guides, round-trip transportation from Anchorage, tents and sleeping bags, all meals, rain gear, paddling accessories and fiberglass kayaks. No kayaking experience is required, and there is space for only 12 people. Two nights lodging in Anchorage is included for …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: 4 easy steps to fix Nelchina permit system Published: March 20th, 2005 Last Modified: March 20th, 2005 at 06:38 AM From all the teeth-gnashing surrounding the hunt for Nelchina caribou, it might seem impossible to devise a fair way to divide a few thousand permits among tens of thousands of hunters. Today, you may recall, we're stuck in a "Tier II subsistence" quagmire. The Tier II system is great if you are in one of the special classes of Nelchina caribou hunters basically ensured permits for life. It's not so great if you are one of the thousands of hunters who would one day like a chance to hunt for Nelchina caribou but can only hope to obtain a permit in the next 20 years -- if you're lucky. But there's really an easy, four-step solution to the Nelchina problem. • Step o…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Breakup's coming so Ice Classic's under way Published: March 8th, 2005 Last Modified: March 8th, 2005 at 02:50 AM In the annual Nenana Ice Classic contest, now in its 89th year, Alaskans attempt to guess the minute when the ice will go out on the Tanana River. A tripod will signal the moment of breakup when it falls. Tickets good for one guess each are $2.50 and are sold through April 5 at locations around the state, including Carrs stores in Anchorage. For more information, see www.nenanaakiceclassic.com . related more Jeff Mayrand of Nenana pounds a spike into the side of the Nenana Ice Classic tripod Sunday on the Tanana River near Nenana. title: 'Poetry in motion' at Telemark festival
summary: Tim Charnon zigzagged gracefully across the finish line, a smile on his face. There was no tension in this race. Skiers had put on skins, raced up Mount Alyeska, skate-skied across the ridge and then descended in telltale telemark lunges. No one knew who'd come in first -- and no one cared. The second annual Jeff Nissman Telepalooza telemark festival was a weekend of remembrance, not results. It was a time for backcountry skiers to showcase their sport while celebrating the life of a departed friend. Nissman died in January 2004 when a giant slab of ice slid off a U.S. Forest Service building roof and landed on him. It was not the way one would expect a 29-year-old man like Nissman to leave this world. His adventures took him to Australia, Ecuador and all over the Alaska backcountry. He wa…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Iditarod Trail Invitational biker tore up old time in unsung feat RECORD: Mike Curiak pedaled trail from Knik to McGrath in 3 days, 6 hours. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: April 3rd, 2005 Last Modified: April 3rd, 2005 at 06:14 AM Lost in the hubbub surrounding the Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race this year was a remarkable performance of human, not canine, proportions. Coloradan Mike Curiak pedaled a mountain bike along the trail from Knik to McGrath in three days, six hours to set a record for the Iditarod Trail Invitational. Not long ago, it took dog teams that long to cover the 350 miles to McGrath. Even now, on a shorter and easier course from Willow, it takes the race leaders about 2 1/2 days to get there. While some of those same ride-along mushers were grabbing headlin…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Embrace those spring snowstorms By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: April 3rd, 2005 Last Modified: April 3rd, 2005 at 08:29 AM Our best skiing of the season starts now. Where the snow is packed and survives on the Nordic trails, it is warm and fast. And in the mountains, where powder still accumulates, there will soon be that smooth and forgiving corn snow of spring. For all of you sucker-punched last week by the snowstorm that buried the idea that the green season might come early this year, all I can do is offer sympathy. No doubt the end-of-March reminder that Alaska is a winter wonderland hit hard after an oddly mild winter of rain and warmth. Newcomers can be forgiven any thoughts that the unusual winter might spawn unusually early opportunities for hiking, b…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: State biologist peeks into the life of Valley's rainbow trout STUDY: Scientist wants to know what effects fall fishery's popularity will have. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 7th, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 11:40 AM PALMER -- There I was, wading a lovely Susitna Valley stream, ducking ferns and alders that hung over the banks, and catching rainbow trout big enough to bend my light rod like a broom straw. All I had to do, it seemed, was drop my fly into the riffles or along a dark run formed by a fallen tree and -- BAM! -- I was into another leaping, thick-shouldered trout. The early September air was spiced with the smell of ripe berries and each fish seemed a living gem, electrically charged and hand-painted by an artful creator. In the back of my m…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Toothy predators strike hard, then tire FISHING REPORT: Pike may be unwanted by state biologists, but many anglers love to stalk them. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 7th, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 11:38 AM NANCY LAKE STATE RECREATION AREA -- The bass popper plopped into the muskeg pond between shoreline and a clump of lily pads. For a moment it rested there, floating on the coffee-colored water like a little green-and-black frog. Just then, a cloud blocked the sun, and a robin that had been singing its heart out fell silent. A deathly hush swept over the pond. Then it happened. Abruptly as a jack-in-the box, a creature burst from the waters with a violent splash. Resembling a cross between an alligator and a snake, it grabbed the popper in its jaws…

Retired and enjoying her first trip to Alaska, she had never in her life caught a silver salmon. A few minutes spent fishing the salty green swells of Resurrection Bay would quickly change that. title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Dollies, trout await if you go the distance BE CAREFUL: With plentiful fish and few anglers, bears might be lurking closer than you think. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 2nd, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 10:55 AM UPPER RUSSIAN RIVER -- Daily News sportswriter Kevin Klott had just lost the biggest rainbow trout of his life Tuesday when a grizzly came rushing around a bend 20 feet away. The fish had broken 5X tippet and, even as Klott fumed, the bear appeared in a crackling of alders and thumping of heavy paws. For an instant it seemed a collision between the two was imminent. Alaska anglers, forced to accept that they share their streams with bears, often wonder how they will react when they find themselves suddenly nose to nose with a grizzly. Klott, …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Russian teeming with reds PLETHORA: Anglers are catching their limits; the sanctuary opens 6 a.m. today. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 2nd, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 10:58 AM RUSSIAN RIVER -- The air here Tuesday evening was electric. A crisp wind surged, anglers shouted as fishing lines popped and expensive rods broke, and salmon could be seen splashing and leaping just about everywhere. Opening day of fishing on the Russian River had begun at 12:01 a.m. and by all accounts -- including the state's report that afternoon of a whopping 8,763 fresh red salmon through the weir upstream -- the early-season red run was off to one of its strongest starts in recent memory. Outside the Russian River Ferry office, anglers carrying fish filed by like triump…

summary: Outdoors hot line Published: April 3rd, 2005 Last Modified: April 3rd, 2005 at 06:14 AM BOATING APU location for sea kayaking symposium Register now for the 17th annual Alaska Sea Kayaking Symposium May 6-8 at Alaska Pacific University. The weekend event features pool and lake training, lectures and other specialized programs for all levels of kayakers. The cost ranges from $5 per lecture to $75 for a refresher course to $225 for the entire weekend of activities. For more information, go to www.aksks.org or call 275-PADL (7235). title: Wolf-kill efforts proceed for now
summary: An Anchorage judge on Wednesday upheld the state's controversial aerial wolf-kill programs, paving the way for private pilots to shoot more than 600 wolves this winter in rural Alaska. In her decision, Superior Court Judge Sharon Gleason refused to grant an injunction sought by the Connecticut-based group Friends of Animals and six Alaska residents to halt predator-control efforts until their lawsuit against the state goes to trial in May. The same group of plaintiffs sued the state in 2003, saying the Alaska Board of Game and Alaska Department of Fish and Game hadn't justified the predator-program under guidelines in state law. Gleason denied that request. The plaintiffs pitched a similar argument last week, saying the Game Board had not determined the necessary harvest and population fig…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Hiker hit with charges for taking traps SNOW RIVER: Seward man shows trooper where he dumped gear. By TATABOLINE BRANT Anchorage Daily News Published: February 4th, 2005 Last Modified: February 4th, 2005 at 02:42 AM Mark Luttrell detests trapping. And he acknowledges that on a Kenai Peninsula hike recently, his emotions on the issue got the better of him. It was about two weeks ago that Luttrell, 46, parked his vehicle at Mile 13.2 of the Seward Highway and walked back into the Snow River drainage, a broad river valley flanked by steep peaks that's popular among hunters and hikers alike. It was cold, around zero, and the ground was covered with snow. Luttrell said he was enjoying the exercise and scenery when he came upon a cluster of animal snares and traps. The traps -- 20 snares, 5 leg-…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fall means big 'bows are biting NEED TO FEED: Fish try to fatten up before winter. By Ken Marsh Daily News Reporter Published: September 7th, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 12:11 PM They were everything Alaska rainbow trout should be: hard-fighting, leaping things, wild as the tumbling mountain stream where I'd found them. They were big, powerful fish -- three to four pounds apiece on average, with some twice that size -- the kind people pay thousands of dollars to catch at remote Southwest Alaska lodges. Yet these trophies were lurking in waters off Southcentral's road system. And any angler with a little imagination, a few hours of time and a tank full of gas can find them. Capricious weather, highway construction and visions of heavy state fair traffic almost kept me off th…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Steelhead are last fix for fanatical fisherman THRILLING: Hooking into a sea-run rainbow is as good as it gets. By Ken Marsh Anchorage Daily News Published: September 7th, 2004 Last Modified: September 15th, 2004 at 12:08 PM September is a fickle month, clear and frosty one morning, wet and cold the next. As the season works through this bipolar phase, caught between brilliant fall color and the raw misery of autumn rains, it is tempting for anglers to hang up their tackle and call it a summer. Yet for a small subgroup -- a stubborn bunch of hard-core anglers -- this final hour is the season's best. Each September, these folks trade in their lightweight, breathable GoreTex waders of summer for thick, warm neoprenes. They give up T-shirts for polypro long johns, and sunscreen for fingerless…
